info.title: ronutz tools API
info.summary: Deterministic network and security computations over HTTP.
info.description:
The programmatic counterpart to the in-browser tools at ronutz.com.

Every operation here is a pure function: the same input always yields the
same output, nothing is stored, and no request bodies or query values are
logged. The browser tools remain the zero-egress default for sensitive work;
this API exists for automation (scripts, pipelines, integrations).

The surface grows with the toolbox: every shipped tool is exposed here and
documented from the same registry, so the served endpoints and this document
cannot drift apart. Only shipped tools are listed (no vaporware). Each
operation accepts its input via GET (?input=) or POST (the request body) and
returns a JSON result.

servers.description: Production edge (Cloudflare Worker, stateless, no request logging)

get.description: Encode text to Base64, URL-safe Base64, Base32, hexadecimal, or percent-encoding, and decode any of them back. Tolerant of missing padding and whitespace, and it flags binary (non-UTF-8) results. Runs entirely in your browser. Standards: RFC 4648, RFC 3986. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

400.description: The input is missing or could not be processed.

post.description: Encode text to Base64, URL-safe Base64, Base32, hexadecimal, or percent-encoding, and decode any of them back. Tolerant of missing padding and whitespace, and it flags binary (non-UTF-8) results. Runs entirely in your browser. Standards: RFC 4648, RFC 3986. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

400.description: The input is missing or could not be processed.

get.description: Work out a TLS certificate's validity, whether it fits the CA/Browser Forum 47-day schedule, and the renewal cadence it implies — all offline. The input is a JSON object: send it as the request body (POST) or as a JSON string (?input=). Standards: CA/Browser Forum SC-081v3, RFC 5280, RFC 8555. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: The tool input, as a JSON string.

400.description: The input is missing or could not be processed.

post.description: Work out a TLS certificate's validity, whether it fits the CA/Browser Forum 47-day schedule, and the renewal cadence it implies — all offline. The input is a JSON object: send it as the request body (POST) or as a JSON string (?input=). Standards: CA/Browser Forum SC-081v3, RFC 5280, RFC 8555. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

get.summary: Break down an IPv4 CIDR block
get.description:
Computes the network and broadcast addresses, usable host range, host counts, netmask, and wildcard mask for an IPv4 CIDR block.
Edge cases follow the common conventions: a `/31` is treated as an RFC 3021 point-to-point link (2 usable addresses, no separate broadcast), and a `/32` is a single host (1 usable address). For `/0`–`/30`, the network and broadcast addresses are excluded from the usable host count.

parameters.name: block
parameters.description: An IPv4 CIDR block, host bits optional (they are masked off).

400.description: The input is missing or not a valid IPv4 CIDR block.

example.message: Not a valid IPv4 CIDR block. Expected something like 192.168.1.0/24.

get.description: Enter a TLS cipher suite, as an IANA name, an OpenSSL or GnuTLS name, or a hex code point, to break it into its key exchange, authentication, cipher, mode, and MAC, with a plain-language security read-out and the official IANA recommendation. Runs entirely in your browser against a bundled copy of the IANA registry. Standards: IANA TLS Cipher Suites, RFC 8446, RFC 5246, RFC 8447, RFC 7465. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

400.description: The input is missing or could not be processed.

Runs entirely in your browser against a bundled copy of the IANA registry. Standards: IANA TLS Cipher Suites, RFC 8446, RFC 5246, RFC 8447, RFC 7465. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

get.description: Walk a packet through AFM's documented context order and watch the semantics that decide real outcomes: accept passes one context and the traffic is processed again at the next, only accept-decisively ends the walk, ICMP rules at a virtual server or self IP are ignored, and staged policies log without enforcing. A lone policy gets the redundant-and-conflicting audit the system itself defines. Standards: F5 BIG-IP AFM Network Firewall. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

examples: security firewall policy P { rules { r1 { action drop } } }

400.description: The input is missing or could not be processed.

post.description: Walk a packet through AFM's documented context order and watch the semantics that decide real outcomes: accept passes one context and the traffic is processed again at the next, only accept-decisively ends the walk, ICMP rules at a virtual server or self IP are ignored, and staged policies log without enforcing. A lone policy gets the redundant-and-conflicting audit the system itself defines. Standards: F5 BIG-IP AFM Network Firewall. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

examples: security firewall policy P { rules { r1 { action drop } } }

400.description: The input is missing or could not be processed.

get.description: The Session Variables chapter as a pattern-aware lookup: paste session.ad.last.attr.memberOf and it resolves against the chapter's own templates, paste an mcget expression and every reference inside is explained with the secure audit riding along, the classic empty-value trap named exactly: a bare mcget on session.logon.last.password reads back empty, because secure variables require -secure. Standards: F5 APM session variables. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

400.description: The input is missing or could not be processed.

get.description: The eight SSO methods APM's own chapter defines, each card carrying the verdict that decides outages: a misconfigured SSO object for any non-form method can disable SSO for every method in that user's session; Form Based and Forms - Client Initiated are the only exempt ones. Kerberos ships its full prerequisite list, including the line worth framing: APM Kerberos SSO does not need or use a keytab file. Standards: F5 APM Single Sign-On Methods. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

get.description: Paste a BIG-IP Advanced WAF declarative security policy (JSON) and get a section-by-section, plain-language reading grounded in F5's published schema, with security callouts that read the values: transparent enforcement means monitor-only, plus signature staging, Data Guard off, and cookies missing Secure or HttpOnly. Runs entirely in your browser. Standards: F5 BIG-IP Advanced WAF declarative policy (schema v17.1). Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

get.description: Assemble a BIG-IP-correct tcpdump command from structured choices: the TMM interface syntax, flow detail, snaplen, file output, and a BPF filter. It formats the command for you to run; it captures nothing. The input is a JSON object: send it as the request body (POST) or as a JSON string (?input=). Standards: tcpdump, F5 TMM :nnn capture syntax. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

parameters.description: The tool input, as a JSON string.

examples: interface 0.0, high detail, both sides, filtered by host

get.description: Every AFM DoS vector explained in F5's own words, with the threshold mechanics spelled out and the configuration cross-checked: the silent mitigation-below-detection inversion, automatic-mode semantics, policing without detection, and the SYN-cookie interplay. Defensive configuration only. Standards: F5 AFM DoS profiles. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

examples: security dos device-config dos-device-config { dos-device-vector { tcp-half-open { } } }

get.description: Per when block, an honest verdict: expressible in an LTM policy (with a migration sketch in the vendor's own example grammar), verify on your version, or iRule-required with the blockers named. Policies are the no-programming layer; this tool tells you which blocks belong there. Standards: F5 LTM policies, F5 iRules. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

get.description: The protocol-profile decision in cards: full-proxy tcp with every feature, the living f5-tcp-* four that F5 continually updates (read-only, tuned via child profiles) versus the frozen legacy -optimized trio, FastL4's PVA packet path with the loose pair for asymmetric routing, and FastHTTP's narrow-but-fast HTTP case with its complete when-to-use criteria list and K8024 as required reading. Standards: F5 LTM protocol profiles. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

get.description: Paste a one-connect profile for the full option audit with the man page's own semantics and defaults, or simulate a mask against real client IPs and watch the reuse groups form. The marquee demonstration: SNAT translates first, so a single SNAT address collapses every client into one reuse group no matter how narrow the mask. Standards: F5 tmsh ltm profile one-connect, F5 K7208 / K5911. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

get.description: Topology decisions computed the way BIG-IP DNS computes them: the Longest Match sort with per-record rationale, the scoring walk with shadowing shown, highest score wins, ties round-robin. Standards: F5 GTM topology records. Stateless and side-effect-free: the same input always yields the same result, and nothing is stored or logged.

CidrResult.description: The full breakdown of an IPv4 CIDR block.

input.description: The input echoed back in canonical dotted-quad form. Host bits are preserved here; see `network` for the masked base address.

broadcast.description: The broadcast address. For a /31 or /32 this equals the upper address.

netmask.description: The subnet mask in dotted-decimal form.

wildcard.description: The inverse (wildcard) mask, as used in ACLs.

totalAddresses.description: Total addresses in the block (2 ^ host bits).

usableHosts.description: Addresses assignable to hosts. For /0–/30 this excludes the network and broadcast addresses; /31 yields 2 and /32 yields 1.

Error.description: A structured error. No request input is echoed beyond what is needed to explain the problem.

examples: Not a valid IPv4 CIDR block. Expected something like 192.168.1.0/24.

ToolResult.description: The tool's computed result as a JSON object. The exact fields depend on the tool; see the tool's page at https://ronutz.com for the full shape. The result is a pure function of the input, so the same input always yields the same output.
